availableCommands.name: aspnet-core
availableCommands.description: Build, review, refactor, or architect ASP.NET Core web applications using current official guidance for .NET web development. Use when working on Blazor Web Apps, Razor Pages, MVC, Minimal APIs, controller-based Web APIs, SignalR, gRPC, middleware, dependency injection, configuration, authentication, authorization, testing, performance, deployment, or ASP.NET Core upgrades. (user)

availableCommands.name: deep-research
availableCommands.description: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. (dynamic workflow)

availableCommands.name: design-sync
availableCommands.description: Push a React design system to claude.ai/design. This runs a converter that bundles the real component code (from Storybook or a bare package) and uploads it. Use when the user runs /design-sync or says "sync my design system to Claude Design".

availableCommands.name: dataviz
availableCommands.description: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

availableCommands.name: update-config
availableCommands.description: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

availableCommands.name: verify
availableCommands.description: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

availableCommands.name: debug
availableCommands.description: Enable debug logging for this session and help diagnose issues

availableCommands.name: code-review
availableCommands.description: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

availableCommands.name: simplify
availableCommands.description: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

availableCommands.name: batch
availableCommands.description: Research and plan a large-scale change, then execute it in parallel across 5–30 isolated worktree agents that each open a PR.

availableCommands.name: fewer-permission-prompts
availableCommands.description: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

availableCommands.name: loop
availableCommands.description: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace.

availableCommands.name: claude-api
availableCommands.description:
Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).

availableCommands.name: run
availableCommands.description: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

file.content:
# Project Overview

Emdash is a local-first, cross-platform Electron app for running multiple AI coding
agents in parallel. Each task is isolated in its own Git worktree and can run locally
or over SSH, while the desktop app coordinates provider CLIs, ACP chat sessions,
terminal sessions, issue and PR integrations, diff review, and release packaging.

## Repository Structure

This is a pnpm workspace monorepo. The Electron app lives in
`apps/emdash-desktop/` as `@emdash/emdash-desktop`; shared packages live under
`packages/`. Unless a path is prefixed with `packages/` or another app, app paths
such as `src/...`, `drizzle/`, `scripts/`, and `build/` are relative to
`apps/emdash-desktop/`.

Repo root:

- `.claude/` - Local Claude agent settings for this checkout.
- `.github/` - GitHub issue templates, reusable actions, CI, and release workflows.
- `agents/` - Agent-facing architecture, workflow, convention, integration, and risk docs.
- `apps/emdash-desktop/` - The Electron desktop app.
- `packages/chat-ui/` - Shared transcript and ACP chat renderer with Storybook coverage.
- `packages/core/` - Transport-agnostic runtime primitives, including ACP session logic.
- `packages/plugins/` - Agent provider plugin definitions, hooks, and ACP adapters.
- `packages/shared/` - Shared primitives such as result types, logging, and markdown helpers.
- `packages/ui/` - Shared React UI components, theme tokens, recipes, and primitives.
- `pnpm-workspace.yaml` - Workspace package globs for `apps/*` and `packages/**`.
- Root config files - `package.json`, `nx.json`, `.nvmrc`, `.oxfmtrc.json`,
  `.oxlintrc.json`, and lockfile/configuration owned at the workspace root.

Inside `apps/emdash-desktop/`:

- `build/` - Electron packaging assets; avoid edits unless working on packaging/signing.
- `drizzle/` - Generated Drizzle SQL migrations and metadata.
- `scripts/` - Release, verification, and build support scripts.
- `src/main/` - Electron main process, RPC controllers, services, DB, ACP, PTY, and SSH.
- `src/preload/` - Typed Electron preload bridge exposed to the renderer.
- `src/renderer/` - React app organized around `app/`, `features/`, `lib/`, and tests.
- `src/shared/` - App IPC primitives, provider metadata, events, ACP, MCP, skills, and types.
- `src/types/` - Ambient and cross-cutting TypeScript declarations.
- `tooling/` - App-level development and test infrastructure not bundled into production.

## Build & Development Commands

Use Node `24.14.0` from `.nvmrc` and `pnpm@10.28.2`. Root scripts are powered by Nx
and run package targets in dependency order with local caching where configured.

Install dependencies from the repo root:

```bash

`ui`) under `packages/`
- **Repo root** contains `.claude/`, `.github/` (workflows), `agents/`
(architecture docs), and workspace configuration (`pnpm-workspace.yaml`, `package.json`, `nx.json`)
- **App internals** (`apps/emdash-desktop/`) include main process (`src/main/`), React renderer

content.text: I found

it! The definition is at:
`packages/core/src/acp/agent

-update.ts:122`

The grep output shows `export function toAgentUpdate(update: SessionUpdate): AgentUpdate {` at
line 122 in that file.
**`packages/core/src/acp/agent-update.ts:122`**
Read `packages/core/src/acp/agent-update.ts` and `packages/core/src/acp/session-machine.ts`, then explain in two sentences how they relate.
The
user wants me to read two files and explain how they relate in two sentences. Let me read both files.
Now
I need to understand how these two files relate. Let

me analyze:

1. **agent-update.ts**: Defines
the `AgentUpdate` type (a discriminated union with kinds like 'message', 'thinking
', 'tool_call', 'tool_update', 'plan', 'ignored') and the `to
AgentUpdate()` function that converts raw ACP SessionUpdate objects into the provider-neutral

AgentUpdate vocabulary.

2. **session-machine.ts**: Implements a pure A
CP session state machine with pure functions `decide()` and `evolve()`, plus
a stateful `SessionMachine` class. The state machine processes Commands (user
intents) and DomainEvents (facts from the agent). Looking
at the `DomainEvent` type, I see there's an `Updated` event that carries an

`AgentUpdate`: `{ type: 'Updated'; update: AgentUpdate }`.

So the relationship is:
- `agent-update.ts` defines `
AgentUpdate` and the conversion function to get there from

raw ACP updates
- `session-machine.ts` uses `AgentUpdate` in its DomainEvent type, specifically

the `Updated` event that feeds updates through the state machine's `evolve()` function

The connection is
that the raw ACP `SessionUpdate` is converted to `AgentUpdate` by `toAgentUpdate()`, then that

`AgentUpdate` is wrapped in a DomainEvent and fed into the session machine's state machine logic.

Let me write
this in two sentences.
`agent-update.ts` defines the
`AgentUpdate` type and exports the `toAgentUpdate()` function that converts raw ACP protocol messages into a provider-neutral vocabulary. `session-machine.ts` uses `
AgentUpdate` in its `Updated` domain event to feed those converted updates through the pure
state machine's `evolve()` function, which folds them into the session state and produces effects for the runtime
to perform.

content.text: Make a checklist / todo plan for adding an optional `foo: string` field to AgentUpdate. Do NOT edit any files.

Here's a checklist for adding an optional `foo: string` field to `AgentUpdate`. No files edited.

## Plan:

add optional `foo?: string` to `AgentUpdate`

**1. Decide scope of the field**
- [ ] Confirm which variants get `foo`
— a single variant (e.g. `message`), a subset, or all of the union in `pack
ages/core/src/acp/agent-update.ts:43`. Optional fields on
a discriminated union must be added per-variant, not once, so this decision drives the rest.

**2. Amend the type**
- [ ] Add `foo?: string;` to the chosen variant(s) in the `AgentUpdate` union (`agent-

update.ts:43-93`).
- [ ] Update the doc comment above the union / relevant variant to explain what `foo` means and when

it's populated.

**3. Populate it in `toAgentUpdate`**
- [ ] In `toAgentUpdate` (`agent-update.
ts:122`), set `foo` in the matching `case` block(s) — decoding it from the ACP `SessionUpdate` if
it comes from the protocol, or leaving it unset if it's only populated later by provider `enrich`.
- [ ] If `foo` originates from vendor `_meta` (like `parentToolCallId`), leave it `

undefined` in the baseline and note that `IAcpBehavior.enrich` promotes it.

**4.

Provider enrichment (if applicable)**
- [ ] Check `packages/core/src/agents/plugins/capabilities/
acp.ts` (the `enrich` hook, ~line 85) and any provider `enrich` implementations under `packages/plugins/

` to populate `foo` if it's vendor-specific.

**5. Downstream consumers**
- [ ] `packages/core/src/acp/session-machine.
ts` — `foo` flows through `Updated` events and `update` Effects unchanged (no code change needed, but verify no ex

haustive destructuring drops it).
- [ ] Trace `AgentUpdate` consumers: `src/renderer/features
/tasks/acp/` (maps updates into `@emdash/chat-ui`)
and `packages/chat-ui/` renderer — decide whether `foo` needs rendering or

just passthrough.
- [ ] Check `SessionSnapshot` / IPC serialization path (`state.ts`) — an

optional string serializes fine, but confirm no schema/validation rejects unknown fields.

**6

. Tests**
- [ ] Update/add cases in `packages/core/src/acp/agent-update.test.ts` covering `foo` set

and unset.
- [ ] Check `acp-session-runtime.test.ts` and any chat-ui snapshot tests for fixtures that ass

ert on full `AgentUpdate` shape.

**7. Verify**
- [ ] `pnpm run typecheck` (

catches every exhaustive switch/spread that needs updating).
- [ ]

`pnpm run test` (or focused `@emdash/core` + `chat-ui` targets).
- [ ]

`pnpm run lint` / `pnpm run format`.

The two load-bearing spots are the union definition and
`toAgentUpdate` (both in `agent-update.ts`); everything else is passthrough plus de
ciding whether the renderer should surface `foo`.

on a strict separation: **Commands** are validated intents that may be rejected (

content.text: via `decide`), while **DomainEvents** are facts that are always folded into state (via `evolve`). Both are

pure functions with no I/O — they emit `Effect`s that the runtime interprets.

There are six phases:

`starting`, `replaying`, `ready`, `working`, `cancelling`, and `closed`.

## `starting`

The initial phase for
every session (`initialMachineState`). The session has been created but neither established n

or replayed yet. Two mutually exclusive exits:

- **`newSession` path** → `SessionRe

ady` event → `ready`. Used when starting fresh; the session becomes immediately usable.
- **
`loadSession` path** → `ReplayStarted` event → `replaying`. Used when a prior session is being restored and

its transcript must be replayed first.

Both setup events may carry `modes` and `configOptions`
; a `meta` effect is emitted alongside `state` only when that metadata is actually present (`needsMeta

Effect`).

## `replaying`

Reached only on the `loadSession` path. A synthetic replay tur
n (`source: 'replay'`) is opened via `makeTurn` to accumulate the historical updates stre

aming back from the agent.

- `SessionLoaded` may arrive here to fold in session metadata (modes/config

) without changing phase.
- `ReplayEnded` commits the replay turn with its final status and transitions to `ready`.

## `ready`

The idle, usable state — the only phase where a new prompt is accepted. `is
PromptReady` returns `true` exclusively for `ready`, and `canSubmit` additionally requires no pending perm

ission.

- A `Prompt` command (valid only here) produces `PromptStarted` → `working`.
- Metadata changes (`MetaChanged`

) can update modes, config options, available commands, and usage figures without leaving `ready`.

## `working

`

An in-flight turn is active. `PromptStarted` opens a live turn (`source: 'live'`) whose first update is the synthes

ized user message, and emits an `agentEvent` with phase `start`.

- **`Updated`** events append
streamed agent updates to the active turn and emit `update` effects. Crucially, `Updated` never flips the l

ifecycle — it stays `working`.
- **`TurnEnded`** commits the turn (status `complete`
/`cancelled`/`error` derived from the outcome), records `lastStopReason`, and returns to `ready`, em

itting an `agentEvent` of `stop` or `error`.
- **`Cancel`** command → `CancellationRequested` → `cancelling`

.

## `cancelling`

The user requested cancellation but the agent hasn't acknowledged the turn's end yet. On ent
ering, all pending permission requests are drained with `cancelled: true`. The phase resolves when `TurnEnded` arrives

, transitioning back to `ready`.

## `closed`

Terminal. Reached from `ready`, `working`,
or `cancelling` when `ProcessClosed` fires (agent process exited / connection dropped).
Any still-active turn is committed as `status: 'error'`, pending permissions are drained as cancelled, and `closed` + `state` effects are emitted with

the exit code.

## Permissions (orthogonal to phase)

Permission handling spans phases r
ather than being one: `PermissionRequested` appends to `pendingPermissions`; `PermissionResolved` removes one

; and both `CancellationRequested` and `ProcessClosed` drain all pending requests as cancelled.

## Example event sequence

A typical `loadSession` restore followed by one user turn:

```text
Phase: starting
  → E: ReplayStarted

⇒ phase: replaying   [effect: state]
  → E: SessionLoaded {modes, config

}     ⇒ phase: replaying   [effect: meta]
  → E: Updated (historical agent_message)

⇒ phase: replaying  [effect: update]
  → E: ReplayEnded {status: complete}    ⇒ phase: ready

[effects: turnCommitted, state]

Phase: ready
  → C: Prompt {userUpdate}               ⇒ (

decide ok) 
  → E: PromptStarted {userUpdate}        ⇒ phase: working     [effects: state, update

, agentEvent:start]

Phase: working
  → E: Updated (agent_message_chunk)     ⇒ phase: working     [effect: update]

→ E: PermissionRequested {req-1}       ⇒ phase: working     [effect: permissionRequest]
  → C: ResolvePermission {req

-1, allow}  ⇒ (decide ok)
  → E: PermissionResolved {req-1}        ⇒ phase: working     [effect: permissionResolved]
  →

E: Updated (tool_call_update)        ⇒ phase: working     [effect: update]
  → E: TurnEnded {stopped,

end_turn}     ⇒ phase: ready       [effects: turnCommitted, state, agentEvent:stop]

Phase: ready
  → E

: ProcessClosed {exitCode: 0}       ⇒ phase: closed      [effects: state, closed]
```
Note how `decide` gates the `Prompt`/`ResolvePerm
ission` **commands** (rejecting out-of-phase intents with an `AcpRuntimeError`
and no state change), while `evolve` unconditionally folds the **events**, and an out-of-place event would yield only a diagnostic `warn`

effect rather than an error.

Separately — the background subagent finished its `stopReason` file search; want me to rel
ay its summary?
